macro_events.event_summary: Iran has reportedly closed the Strait of Hormuz again, firing on a vessel attempting to transit — threatening ~20% of global seaborne oil flows.
primary_drivers: Oil supply disruption risk → crude price spike
Shipping route disruption → tanker rates spike and insurance costs surge
Geopolitical risk premium → defense spending expectations rise, safe-haven flows

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large upstream production outside the Gulf; every $10 move in Brent adds materially to earnings. Limited direct Hormuz transit exposure vs. Gulf-heavy peers.
US-heavy upstream (Permian, Gulf of Mexico) benefits from higher crude without proportional Hormuz operational risk. Direct leverage to Brent spike.
Pure-play Permian producer — zero Middle East exposure, maximum leverage to US WTI price surge as Hormuz shutdown forces buyers toward non-Gulf barrels.
Largest US natgas producer; Hormuz closure disrupts Qatari LNG (~20% of global LNG) shipping, sending global gas prices higher and lifting Henry Hub via LNG export arbitrage.
Frontline tanker fleet — VLCC/Suezmax day rates spike dramatically when Hormuz routes are disrupted; rerouting around Africa doubles ton-miles. Direct rate beneficiary.
Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; Qatari LNG supply disruption through Hormuz drives premium pricing for Gulf Coast cargoes and boosts spot margins.
Defense prime with direct Patriot/THAAD/missile-defense exposure; Gulf escalation typically accelerates FMS orders from Saudi/UAE and raises US supplemental defense spending odds.
Raytheon missile defense (SM-3, Patriot) and Pratt engines see accelerated demand in Gulf conflict scenarios; also benefits from Navy/carrier group deployment replenishment.

losers.rationale: Delta has meaningful international long-haul exposure and is highly sensitive to jet fuel prices — no fuel hedges in recent years, so crude spike hits margins directly within the quarter.
United's transpacific/Europe-India routes face both fuel cost shock and potential airspace rerouting around Iran; jet fuel is ~25% of opex with minimal hedging.
Carnival bunker fuel costs spike with crude; also consumer discretionary demand sensitive to pump prices. Double hit on cost and demand.
American Airlines has the weakest balance sheet among US majors — fuel cost shock compounds leverage concerns. Historically the highest-beta name to oil spikes.
FedEx Express air network is jet-fuel intensive; Hormuz-driven crude spike raises costs faster than contractual fuel surcharges can recapture.
Dow's ethylene/petrochemical feedstock costs rise with naphtha/ethane linkage to crude; margin compression as price pass-through lags in contracted volumes.
Walmart's low-income consumer base is acutely sensitive to gasoline prices — every $0.25 at the pump historically compresses discretionary basket size. Also diesel hits inbound logistics costs.

headlines: Iran says Strait of Hormuz is closed again as vessel attempting to cross comes under gunfire

macro_events.event_summary: Iran war and $4 gasoline are crimping US consumer discretionary spending, with a narrowing window to revive it before the economy deteriorates further.
primary_drivers: Elevated gasoline prices acting as a tax on discretionary income
Geopolitical risk premium in oil keeping energy costs high
Consumer sentiment deterioration compressing discretionary spend

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with direct upstream leverage to elevated crude prices from Iran conflict risk premium; large US production base insulates from Middle East supply disruption.
Pure-play US E&P with highest torque to sustained crude price levels; no downstream drag, direct beneficiary of geopolitical risk premium.
Defensive consumer staples retailer that gains share when pinched consumers trade down from discretionary retailers and restaurants; grocery/value mix benefits from stagflationary consumer behavior.
Dollar store model directly benefits from lower-income consumer stress and gas-price-driven trade-down; historically outperforms in gas-price-shock periods.
Prime defense contractor with direct exposure to increased DoD munitions/missile demand from active Iran conflict (PAC-3, THAAD, JASSM).
Permian-weighted producer with high oil beta; geopolitical premium flows directly to cash flow given unhedged US-heavy production.

losers.rationale: Cruise operator hit on both sides: fuel is a major cost input with limited ability to pass through, and discretionary travel demand softens as consumers pull back; also direct Middle East itinerary disruption.
Airline with high jet fuel sensitivity and leisure travel exposure; $4 gas correlates with consumer travel pullback while fuel costs compress margins.
Discretionary fast-casual dining with premium price points loses traffic when gas prices tax low/mid-income wallets; historically sees comp deceleration in gas-shock periods.
Royal Caribbean shares Carnival's fuel cost pressure and discretionary travel demand risk; Middle East/Mediterranean itinerary rerouting adds operational cost.
Discretionary-heavy merchandise mix (apparel, home) makes Target more exposed than Walmart to gas-price-squeezed consumer; recent quarters already showed traffic sensitivity to fuel.
Las Vegas Sands reliant on discretionary gaming/leisure spend which contracts first in consumer slowdowns; Asia exposure adds geopolitical overhang.
Auto OEM exposed to both weakening discretionary big-ticket demand and consumer shift away from lower-MPG trucks/SUVs where Ford has highest margin mix.
Online travel agency revenue tied to discretionary travel booking volume; high gas prices and war uncertainty directly suppress leisure trip planning.

headlines: Iran war, $4 gas, took all the fun out of the U.S. consumer economy. There's only so much time to bring it back

macro_events.event_summary: Trump's expected Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh is reportedly aligned with cutting interest rates, signaling a more dovish Fed regime ahead.
primary_drivers: Lower expected policy rate path → lower discount rate on long-duration cash flows
Steeper yield curve / lower front-end rates benefit rate-sensitive sectors
Weaker USD implied by dovish Fed pivot

beneficiaries.rationale: Homebuilders are a pure rate-duration play — mortgage rates track the 10Y and dovish Fed expectations compress them, directly boosting affordability and order volume. D.R. Horton is the largest US builder with highest entry-level exposure.
High-multiple growth name where valuation is dominated by terminal value — direct beneficiary of lower discount rate assumptions in DCF.
Highly levered consumer finance / auto retailer; lower rates reduce floorplan financing costs and boost auto loan affordability for subprime buyers.

losers.rationale: Large money-center banks face net interest margin compression if the front end is cut aggressively while deposit costs remain sticky; also lose on lower reinvestment yields.
Schwab's earnings are heavily tied to net interest income on client cash sweeps; aggressive front-end cuts directly compress this spread.

headlines: Trump expects his Fed chair nominee to cut interest rates. Here’s how Kevin Warsh might try to do it.

macro_events.event_summary: Op-ed frames post-Iran war outcome as cementing US as the world's most secure energy power, implying sustained US hydrocarbon dominance and likely degraded Iranian/Middle East supply competition.
primary_drivers: Reduced Iranian oil export competition benefits US producers' market share
Narrative-driven rotation into US energy security / domestic E&P names
LNG export demand from allies seeking non-Middle East supply

beneficiaries.rationale: Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; 'America as secure energy power' narrative directly supports long-dated LNG export contracts with European/Asian buyers diversifying from Middle East risk.
ExxonMobil has the largest US Permian footprint plus Guyana; benefits from both reduced Iranian barrel competition and the geopolitical premium on Western-aligned supply.
ConocoPhillips is a near-pure-play US/North America upstream producer — direct beneficiary of narrative favoring domestic barrels over OPEC/Iran supply.
Diamondback Energy is a pure-play Permian producer; US energy-security narrative channels capital into domestic shale names with lowest geopolitical risk.
Largest US natural gas producer; gas-side beneficiary of LNG export thesis and allied demand for US molecules over Qatari/Iranian alternatives.
Valero is a US Gulf Coast refiner — benefits if Iranian crude remains sanctioned/constrained, widening sour crude discounts and protecting domestic refining margins.

headlines: The real outcome of the Iran war: America is now the world’s most secure energy power

macro_events.event_summary: Iran claims the Strait of Hormuz is "under strict control," appearing to de-escalate shipping chokepoint fears while Tesla earnings loom as a separate catalyst.
primary_drivers: De-escalation of Hormuz shipping disruption risk → oil risk premium compression
Lower crude = relief for fuel-cost-sensitive transport/airlines
Reduced tanker rate spike tailwind for shipping names

beneficiaries.rationale: Jet fuel is ~20-25% of airline opex; de-escalation compresses crude risk premium, directly improving near-term margin outlook. Delta has historically traded inversely to Brent on geopolitical shocks.
Same jet fuel sensitivity as DAL; UAL has higher international/long-haul exposure making it more fuel-levered than domestic-heavy peers.
Southwest has unwound most of its fuel hedges in recent years, leaving it more spot-exposed; drop in crude risk premium is a direct P&L tailwind.
Cruise line bunker fuel is a major cost line; Hormuz de-escalation also removes Middle East itinerary disruption risk for Mediterranean/Red Sea routes.

losers.rationale: Integrated major with upstream leverage to Brent; a compression of the Hormuz war premium removes a recent tailwind to crude prices and E&P cash flow expectations.
Pure-play US E&P with high beta to WTI/Brent; geopolitical premium unwind pressures the name disproportionately vs. diversified majors.
Permian pure-play, highly levered to crude spot — loses the geopolitical risk premium bid that had supported recent strength.
Frontline tanker rates spike on Hormuz disruption fears; de-escalation removes the rerouting/scarcity bid for VLCC day rates.
Scorpio Tankers — product tanker rates were bid on Hormuz diversion scenarios; 'strict control' narrative removes that catalyst.

headlines: Dow Jones Futures: Iran Says Hormuz 'Under Strict Control'; Tesla Earnings Due
